🟡 Alliance for Scotland's Rainforest

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A partnership of 25 organizations committed to collaborative action for Scotland's temperate rainforest. Formed to address one of the world's most biodiversity-depleted countries, the Alliance works to restore and double the size of Scotland's rainforest by 2045, creating a more resilient and better functioning ecosystem.

Bioregional Context

Scotland's temperate rainforest zone stretches along the country's western coast and islands, characterized by high rainfall, mild temperatures, and remarkable biodiversity. This globally rare ecosystem—found on only about 1% of the planet's land surface—contains ancient woodlands some more than 8,000 years old, with exceptional moss, liverwort, and lichen communities found in few other places worldwide. An estimated 30,000 hectares of rainforest remain in Scotland—a mere 2% of woodland cover and only a fraction of the area that could support rainforest.

Place
Edinburgh, Scotland, United Kingdom
links
https://savingscotlandsrainforest.org.uk/blog/the-common-ground-forum-what-difference-can-an-award-make|https://thecommongroundforum.scot/|https://www.act-now.org.uk/Handlers/Download.ashx?IDMF=daab8ba6-02a2-44e8-9f3c-4b3cb406e148

Tags

🌱 Vegetation_Planting 🐉 Species_Control 🏵️ Habitat_Connectivity 🔘 Governance_Bodies ♾️ Commons_Governance_Protocols 📚 Training_and_Employment_Pathways ✅ Result_Based_Mechanisms

Background

Scotland's temperate rainforest woodlands are one of its most precious resources, some more than 8,000 years old, and all of them bursting with life. Hundreds of different species are found here in exceptional quantities, many rare elsewhere—from resident jays and woodpeckers to migrant pied flycatchers and wood warblers, from rare pearl-bordered fritillaries to badgers, red squirrels and pine martens hiding among the trees and crags. It is an incredibly rare habitat, found on only about 1% of our planet's land surface, and the west of Scotland is one of those special places with just the right conditions.

Much of Scotland's temperate rainforest has been lost over the centuries, with an estimated 30,000 hectares remaining—a mere 2% of woodland cover. Despite a plethora of environmental organisations working for decades, with thousands of members, hundreds of nature reserves and countless initiatives, Scotland is still one of the most biodiversity-depleted countries on Earth.

That loss runs deeper than the landscape—it has faded from our collective imagination too. When a community engagement officer asked a group of 27 primary-age children if they knew of the Amazon rainforest, most put their hands up. But when asked if they knew about Scotland's rainforest, only two hands went up.

Origins and Network

The Alliance for Scotland's Rainforest (ASR) was formed in 2021 to address this challenge. The Alliance now has 25 diverse partner organisations and works collaboratively with 20 more, all of whom are committed to pooling their resources for the benefit of Scotland's rainforest. Partners include a range of conservation and environmental organisations, specialist societies and government agencies. In 2021, the Scottish Government made a commitment to expand and restore Scotland's rainforest, and then worked closely with ASR to develop a strategic approach to deliver on that commitment, published in 2024.

Much of the work is about coordinating landscape-scale projects, securing long-term funding, building capacity to deliver on-the-ground work, and communicating about the rainforest and the threats it faces. The partners work together with local people to provide the expertise, resource and capacity needed to deliver the vision for Scotland's rainforest.

Activities

🔸

Landscape-Scale Restoration Projects

 🔸

The Common Ground Forum

 🔸

Our Rainforest Futures

 🔸

Nature Finance for Scotland's Rainforest

Vision and Legacy

By 2045, the vision is clear: all of Scotland's rainforest restored, its size doubled, creating a more resilient ecosystem. Local communities will be championing the rainforest, wildlife will be more diverse, and at least 30 landscape-scale projects will be working to fully restore Scotland's rainforest. A £500 million Rainforest Restoration Fund will be established, with every £1 spent on rainforest restoration bringing £2 into the local economy.

Restoring Scotland's rainforest will take decades, and the vision of a healthy network of woodlands across west Scotland is the work of generations. Yet, with so many people and organisations now working together, that possibility feels closer than ever. As one land manager put it: "I've been filled with hope by the number of different people coming together with the aim of saving the rainforest. Our rainforest really could be saved if we continue the momentum!"
